Next steps is to discuss pricing based on the multiple scenarios. Crawl with NU and up to $100M in spend over 18 months Walk with 2 other schools, multiple input and output engines Run in full production, 20+ schools Mind blown with 100 schools in production Time frame: week of June 8 th or 15th. I would anticipate that this will be an initial high-level discussion at first and create general concepts of total costs going forward. More detailed review and contractual discussion when NU signs up for the program. Second is a more hands on, in-depth, review of the tool set We will need to see it in action A site visit of one of your customers Live action with one student as buyer, two as sellers. Walk through the end-to-end process, in a live environment using NU spend data, and select product, put it out to bid. What We Want to See the AI Agent Do: Ingest context from a brief plain-language description of the need (typed or spoken) and generate a structured, category-appropriate RFP draft Automatically populate standard sections: scope of work, technical requirements, service levels, pricing model structure, evaluation criteria, and terms/conditions framework Reference historical category data and market benchmarks (e.g., Staffing Industry Analysts, Bureau of Labor Statistics rates) to inform rate card structure Suggest a recommended supplier shortlist based on category fit, past performance indicators, and diversity/ESG criteria Identify missing information and prompt the user to complete gaps — do not silently skip required fields Allow iterative refinement via conversational AI ("change the pricing structure to time-and-materials only" or "add a cybersecurity-specific SLA section") The goal is to see if the following can be shown Dimension What We Are Evaluating 1. Practical Utility Does the AI agent produce outputs that a seasoned procurement professional would actually use without significant rework? Is the quality of the RFP or negotiation strategy at a CPO-ready standard? 2. Reasoning Transparency Can the evaluator understand why the agent made each decision? Is the AI's logic explainable, auditable, and defensible to a CFO or Board? Hidden black-box logic is disqualifying. 3. Human-AI Teaming Is the handoff between AI action and human decision well-designed? Does the system know what it doesn't know? Are escalation triggers meaningful and configurable? 4. Integration Depth Does the AI agent leverage real organizational data (spend history, supplier master, contract terms) or operate in a generic vacuum? Can it connect to ERPs, market data feeds, or external databases? 5. Risk & Compliance Design Does the system enforce approval thresholds, flag non-compliant terms, maintain audit trails, and respect data privacy requirements? Is it enterprise-grade in its governance architecture? Demonstration of Spend and ANA set for 26th may

metrics.narrative: Roy Anderson has quantified the business case repeatedly and consistently. On the 07 May call he stated Northeastern alone has '$100,000,000 that is literally low hanging fruit that no one has touched' and that at 10-15% savings 'that's $10,000,000 right there.' He frames the funding model as a 2% supplier win-fee on managed spend yielding '$2 million annually, about $1 million for technology and $0.8 million for labor' (07 May). On 09 Jun he set tiered scale targets: pilot $5-10M, then $100M per institution, scaling to $500M and $1B program scenarios across up to 50 university implementations. On 29 Jun he refined that the $100M analysis would phase over a ~12-month window and not all of it is negotiable. The metrics are buyer-stated, specific, and dated.

champion.narrative: Roy Anderson is a strong, motivated, domain-expert champion: a former Zycus-familiar professional (ex-Trade Shift, ran Go Procure) now teaching procurement at Northeastern, who is architecting the entire MSP program and selling it internally ('I told you I would champion it... I've got executive backing on it' - 09 Jun). He drives the agenda, supplies spend data and a supplier master, and is recruiting 10 co-op/grad students for implementation work. The weakness is not his commitment but his position: he is a College Lecturer, not a budget holder, and he is the SOLE buyer-side contact across all five calls. His access to the actual economic buyer is asserted but unproven. 'Developing' applies because advocacy is high but the path to power and a second thread are both still missing.

competition.narrative: This is a primarily greenfield displacement-and-build motion rather than a head-to-head bake-off. The incumbents being displaced are Jagger (sourcing, via the old iQuest/Cyquest lineage) and Workday (upstream), both of which Roy says are failing - he wants to 'kick Jagger out, kick Coupa out' (07 May, also referencing Coupa's supplier-network upsell behavior negatively). The real competitive risk is multi-vendor fragmentation: Roy has long-standing relationships with the relevant stakeholder (Anders Liljevic, orchestration/category management - 'I'm gonna support that effort') and Fair Market (auctions), and intends a multi-vendor MSP where Zycus is one tool among several. He is explicit that 'you're competitive with all the other players.' The wedge is to be the platform standard (spend + iSource + autonomous negotiation Anna), anchored by the September pilot and the co-development relationship. No active rival is running a parallel evaluation.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific, articulated, and owned. Roy described the incumbent stack as failing: 'they do have Jagger, it's not working that well... they implemented Workday, which is not working very well for them... it really requires a whole reboot' (07 May). The procurement function is critically under-resourced: '$1,700,000,000, 3 people. They're not doing anything in that space' (07 May). $100M of indirect/tail and lab spend across 3,000-4,000 suppliers is unmanaged. On 26 May he flagged change management as a bigger concern than technology when integrating multiple intake systems. The pain owner is the university's small procurement team and the new business manager Roy mentioned (unnamed), with Roy as the external catalyst. The compelling event is self-created (build the program now), not a hard external deadline.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are undefined - no MSA/SOW, legal review, or InfoSec process is underway. Two legal threads surfaced: supplier terms-and-conditions / 2% win-fee digital acceptance Roy wants pre-contracted (07 May), and student case-study publishing permissions, where on 09 Jun Arthur committed to consult the legal/contract team and on 29 Jun both agreed to source a citation example for legal. Roy noted Zycus legal 'is gonna look at this from more of an Indian perspective' (29 Jun). The RA front-entity (nonprofit vs third-party) is unresolved and determines who signs. To create visibility, the deal needs the contracting counterparty (RA or Northeastern), legal owners, and a target redline timeline established alongside the POC.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has been engaged or named with budget authority. Roy Anderson (College Lecturer) is driving the vision but is not a $250k budget holder; he referenced a planned meeting 'with the Tish and Shekar next Thursday' to raise the model for a go/no-go (07 May). On 09 Jun he said 'I've got executive backing on it' and that he 'championed it with the Tish' and 'can't get any higher than him,' but no budget holder (CFO, VP Finance, Provost, or VP Procurement) has joined any of the five calls or validated the supplier-funded 2% model. Budget authority sits with university leadership Roy is socializing internally, not yet with the relevant stakeholder a joint call with that budget holder is the rank-1 move.

sources: Avoma calls 26 Aug 2025, 07 May, 26 May, 09 Jun, 29 Jun 2026
decision_process.narrative: The process is visible from the call sequence: introductory reconnect (26 Aug 2025), commercial reconnect (07 May), technical demo (26 May), commercial sync (09 Jun), and detailed pricing (29 Jun) - a tight, fortnightly cadence driven by Roy as the single external thread. The next defined step is an implementation-feasibility call with Zycus' solution consultant (Devika) and implementation lead within 8-11 days of 29 Jun, then a September pilot POC. The approval chain beyond Roy is the gap: he references internal socialization ('Tish and Shekar', executive backing) but no committee, procurement team, IT, or legal has appeared on a call. The novel MSP / Roy Anderson (RA) front-entity structure (nonprofit or third-party) is still to be determined (07 May), adding uncertainty around who contracts.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are well documented from the buyer side. The Salesforce Next Step and the call history lay out a five-dimension AI-agent evaluation: Practical Utility (CPO-ready RFP outputs without rework), Reasoning Transparency (explainable, auditable to a CFO or Board), Human-AI Teaming (meaningful escalation, knows what it doesn't know), Integration Depth (leverages real spend/supplier data, connects to ERPs), and Risk and Compliance Design (approval thresholds, audit trails, data privacy). On the 26 May demo Roy probed the AI foundational model, conflict resolution at high transaction volumes (40,000/quarter), verification methods for complex components, and stressed 'the necessity for a robust audit trail and clear supplier evaluation metrics.' Commercial criteria are explicit: a flexible, supplier-funded 2% model avoiding gain-share audit complexity (07 May).

flags: Deliver the hands-on tool review with live NU spend data and AI agent evaluation to validate against the 5-dimension criteria (Practical Utility, Reasoning Transparency, Human-AI Teaming, Integration Depth, Risk & Compliance) before Fair Market or another point solution locks in. This is the buyer's stated next gate and the key proof point to differentiate Zycus's full-suite platform vs. Fair Market's lightweight auction tool. Schedule site visit and live demo within 3 weeks (by mid-July) to stay on pace for September POC launch.

Position Zycus as the 80%+ platform standard to prevent multi-vendor MSP scope fragmentation. Roy has engaged other vendors (he named a category management and orchestration partner with a long-term relationship on the 9 Jun call and referenced Fair Market for autonomous sourcing) and is structuring a multi-vendor MSP model. The risk is Zycus being positioned as one tool among several rather than the core platform, diluting value capture and creating integration complexity. The wedge is the integrated, AI-native, multi-tenant architecture that can scale to 20+ universities with spend analytics, autonomous sourcing, strategic sourcing (iRFP/RFQ), and AI negotiation in one platform. Deliver the POC (Sep target per 9 Jun call) so successfully that Northeastern standardizes on Zycus for 80%+ of activity (Roy's own estimate on 9 Jun) and the other vendors play complementary edge-case roles. Reinforce this positioning in the commercial discussion on 29 Jun and the on-site deep-dive week of 20 Jul.

Win the July 20 trial on Roy's own scorecard: rehearse the end-to-end agentic RFP flow on NU spend data and pre-build evidence for Reasoning Transparency and Risk/Compliance (audit trail, supplier verification), the two dimensions the buyer pressed hardest on the 26 May demo.

competitors.how_we_win: Lean into Jagger's poor adoption; deliver an easy plug-and-play experience that wins users organically.

competitors.name: Workday (incumbent upstream procurement system)
competitors.quote: Roy: 'They implemented Workday, which is not working very well for them, and their mindset is so messed up that they just recreated what they're already doing… it really requires a whole reboot' (7 May call).
competitors.how_we_win: Displacement target, not a defended incumbent. Position Zycus as the plug-and-play MSP layer that sits on top of Workday (flat-file data exchange, minimal ERP touch per 9 Jun call) and fixes the upstream mess without requiring Workday to be ripped out.

competitors.name: Do Nothing / status quo
competitors.quote: Northeastern has been really slow... I am not hard pedaling it yet
competitors.how_we_win: Use the supplier-funded model (no real cost to schools) and September pilot to remove the inertia barrier.

competitors.name: Do Nothing
competitors.quote: Roy listed 'Do Nothing' as an option if the MSP business case does not gain internal traction or if the Economic Buyer deprioritizes the program versus other university initiatives
competitors.source: Avoma 7 May discovery call, Roy Anderson
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of inaction (manual sourcing cycle time, missed savings, lack of spend visibility) and build a compelling ROI case with Roy that the Economic Buyer and CFO can approve; make the MSP business case undeniable

competitors.name: Multi-vendor MSP fragmentation
competitors.quote: I've got some level of requirement... personal input to be able to support that type of program... it certainly could be play
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the single platform standard (spend + iSource + Anna) anchored by the September pilot; show integration cost of multiple tools.

competitors.name: Jagger
competitors.quote: Roy stated Northeastern currently uses Jagger for sourcing and Workday for upstream procurement, and the MSP model may replace or augment these incumbent systems
competitors.source: Avoma 7 May discovery call, Roy Anderson
competitors.how_we_win: Show that Merlin's AI agent capabilities (RFP generation, supplier shortlisting, iterative conversational refinement) are a generational leap beyond Jagger's manual sourcing workflows; emphasize CPO-ready output quality (Practical Utility criterion)

competitors.name: Workday
competitors.quote: Roy mentioned Workday as the incumbent upstream procurement system; the MSP model is focused on sourcing (downstream of Workday) but integration with Workday will be required
competitors.source: Avoma 7 May discovery call, Roy Anderson
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the AI-powered sourcing layer that integrates with Workday (Integration Depth criterion); demonstrate ERP connectivity and data flow from Workday requisitions into Merlin-driven RFP and supplier selection

competitors.name: Category-management and orchestration partner
competitors.quote: Roy mentioned a long-term relationship with a category-management and orchestration partner (unnamed) on 9 Jun call
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the orchestration platform that integrates category management, sourcing, and negotiation rather than a point solution; demonstrate integration depth and governance architecture in AI agent evaluation

competitors.name: Multi-vendor MSP fragmentation (scope dilution risk)
competitors.quote: Roy: 'it is my anticipation to have one solution, but let's be honest, the universities are gonna force some level of flexibility… some people are gonna come back and say, look, I've been using Cyquest, I love Jaeger, I'm gonna continue to use Jaeger' (9 Jun call). Arthur: 'are you thinking about as RA choosing one and then pushing that one or are you looking to play the field?' Roy's response indicates multi-vendor model is live risk.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the unified RA platform (spend analytics + autonomous sourcing + strategic sourcing + Merlin AI negotiation) that replaces Jagger/Workday and scales across 20+ universities, emphasizing integration simplicity and AI negotiation capability. Engage Economic Buyer to secure platform decision (not multi-vendor fragmentation) and lock in Zycus as the standard before Fair Market or Focal Point secure parallel scopes.

competitors.name: Jagger (via iQuest/Cyquest, incumbent sourcing system)
competitors.quote: Roy: 'Here at Northeastern, they do have Jagger, it's not working that well… my point of view is that Jagger is going to literally fall, die on the vine because no one wants to use it because it's so hard to use' (7 May call).
competitors.how_we_win: Displacement target, not a defended incumbent. Position Zycus as the easy-to-use, AI-driven replacement that students and procurement staff will actually adopt.

competitors.name: Jaggaer (incumbent, via iQuest/Cyquest)
competitors.quote: they do have Jagger, it's not working that well... let's bring Zycus in, kick Jagger out
competitors.how_we_win: Replace failing incumbent with an easy plug-and-play, low-touch flow students can run; prove autonomous sourcing on real NU lab/tail spend at the July 20 trial.

competitors.name: Workday (incumbent upstream procurement)
competitors.quote: they implemented Workday, which is not working very well for them
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the layer that flips awards back into Workday/JDE for invoicing, avoiding a rip-and-replace fight.

competitors.name: Pactam (PACTAM)
competitors.quote: find out where are you on your AI journey... benchmark against PACTAM performance
competitors.how_we_win: AI-negotiation benchmark only; prove Merlin/ANA reasoning transparency against Roy's five criteria at the trial.

competitors.name: Workday (incumbent upstream)
competitors.quote: They implemented Workday, which is not working very well for them
competitors.how_we_win: Integrate (flip awards into Workday/JDE for invoicing) rather than rip-and-replace; minimal-touch data exchange.

hard.ais_why:
• Northeastern has an 'entrepreneurial bent to create a re-usable, scalable model combining spend visibility and tactical tail spend agents' ‚Äî 'tail spend agents' is an explicit agentic concept.
• ANA and Merlin are scoped, aligning with the autonomous tail-spend vision the program described.
• The initiative is still concept-stage (Boston round-table origin, 1:1 follow-up) and aims to leverage a quick win outward, so the strong idea is not yet backed by a committed evaluation.

Risk that IT requires more complex integration (live ERP connection, data privacy review, InfoSec scoping) than Arthur's MSP model assumes, adding time and cost.

deltas.to: Engage Economic Buyer to validate 2% supplier-funded model and secure platform decision (not multi-vendor fragmentation). Roy is structuring a multi-vendor MSP
deltas.subject: Engage Economic Buyer to validate 2% supplier-funded model and secure platform decision (not multi-vendor fragmentation). Roy is structuring a multi-vendor MSP model where Zycus competes for scope share (Fair Market for autonomous sourcing, Focal Point for orchestration) rather than owning the unified RA platform. Without EB validation of the commercial model and platform decision, Zycus risks being positioned as one tool among several, diluting value capture and creating integration/scope risk. Next move: request joint call with EB (CFO, VP Finance, Provost, or VP Procurement) to present ROI and secure commitment, or ask Roy to report outcome of internal alignment meeting with 'Tish and Shekar' (decision-makers per 7 May call, planned for week of 8 Jun, no outcome reported back).

deltas.to: Multi-thread beyond Roy Anderson into procurement (3-person team), finance/EB (to validate ROI), IT (for integration and data privacy scoping), and legal (for s
deltas.subject: Multi-thread beyond Roy Anderson into procurement (3-person team), finance/EB (to validate ROI), IT (for integration and data privacy scoping), and legal (for supplier T&Cs and case-study permissions). Deal is single-threaded through Roy (College Lecturer, does not control $250k+ budget approval, only buyer-side contact across all 4 calls). If Roy loses internal support or leaves, deal stalls. Next move: request introductions to procurement lead, finance/EB, IT, and legal stakeholders, or schedule a broader working session (e.g. at the hands-on deep-dive trial week of 20 Jul) that brings in cross-functional stakeholders.

deltas.to: Deliver hands-on deep-dive trial (scheduled for likely week of 20 Jul per Next Step) and Sep POC ($5M-$10M spend cohort) as proof points to overcome Do Nothing
deltas.subject: Deliver hands-on deep-dive trial (scheduled for likely week of 20 Jul per Next Step) and Sep POC ($5M-$10M spend cohort) as proof points to overcome Do Nothing inertia and lock in Zycus as platform standard before Fair Market or Focal Point secure parallel scopes. Roy noted 'Northeastern has been really slow' and 'I am not hard pedaling it yet' (7 May call), indicating internal inertia is a live risk. The deep-dive trial and POC are the next gates to create urgency and demonstrate value. Next move: finalize pricing scenarios (Arthur committed to send Excel sheet 'within a couple of days' per 9 Jun call, overdue as of 29 Jun) and POC plan (also overdue) this week so Roy can socialize internally ahead of 20 Jul trial.

value.quote: Next Step field: 'The goal is to see if the following can be shown: [table with 5 dimensions: 1. Practical Utility—Does the AI agent produce outputs that a seasoned procurement professional would actually use without significant rework? Is the quality of the RFP or negotiation strategy at a CPO-ready standard? 2. Reasoning Transparency—Can the evaluator understand why the agent made each decision? Is the AI's logic explainable, auditable, and defensible to a CFO or Board? Hidden black-box logic is disqualifying. 3. Human-AI Teaming—Is the handoff between AI action and human decision well-designed? Does the system know what it doesn't know? Are escalation triggers meaningful and configurable? 4. Integration Depth—Does the AI agent leverage real organizational data (spend history, supplier master, contract terms) or operate in a generic vacuum? Can it connect to ERPs, market data feeds, or external databases? 5. Risk & Compliance Design—Does the system enforce approval thresholds, flag non-compliant terms, maintain audit trails, and respect data privacy requirements? Is it enterprise-grade in its governance architecture?]'
value.source: Next Step field (SF), last modified 29 Jun 2026
